verses.text: Absalom gets chariots and runners and rises early to stand at the gate, telling each man with a grievance his cause is good 'but there is no man deputed of the king to hear thee.' He poses as the people's champion. Flatterers win hearts by promising what the truth would not.

Absalom gets chariots and runners and rises early to stand at the gate, telling each man with a grievance his cause is good 'but there is no man deputed of the king to hear thee.' He poses as the people's champion. Flatterers win hearts by promising what the truth would not.

Absalom gets chariots and runners and rises early to stand at the gate, telling each man with a grievance his cause is good 'but there is no man deputed of the king to hear thee.' He poses as the people's champion. Flatterers win hearts by promising what the truth would not.

'Oh that I were made judge,' he sighs, kissing all who come, and 'so Absalom stole the hearts of the men of Israel.' Slowly, deliberately, he undermines his father by feigned concern. Treason often comes not by open attack but by a long, smiling theft of trust.

'Oh that I were made judge,' he sighs, kissing all who come, and 'so Absalom stole the hearts of the men of Israel.' Slowly, deliberately, he undermines his father by feigned concern. Treason often comes not by open attack but by a long, smiling theft of trust.

'Oh that I were made judge,' he sighs, kissing all who come, and 'so Absalom stole the hearts of the men of Israel.' Slowly, deliberately, he undermines his father by feigned concern. Treason often comes not by open attack but by a long, smiling theft of trust.

After four years Absalom asks leave to pay a vow in Hebron, cloaking rebellion in religion. David, suspecting nothing, sends him in peace. Evil loves to dress itself in piety; the most dangerous plots are those wrapped in the language of devotion to God.

After four years Absalom asks leave to pay a vow in Hebron, cloaking rebellion in religion. David, suspecting nothing, sends him in peace. Evil loves to dress itself in piety; the most dangerous plots are those wrapped in the language of devotion to God.

After four years Absalom asks leave to pay a vow in Hebron, cloaking rebellion in religion. David, suspecting nothing, sends him in peace. Evil loves to dress itself in piety; the most dangerous plots are those wrapped in the language of devotion to God.

Absalom sends spies through the tribes: at the trumpet, proclaim him king. The conspiracy grows strong, and even David's counsellor Ahithophel joins it. A revolt long prepared bursts out at once. Quiet disloyalty, given time, hardens into open rebellion.

Absalom sends spies through the tribes: at the trumpet, proclaim him king. The conspiracy grows strong, and even David's counsellor Ahithophel joins it. A revolt long prepared bursts out at once. Quiet disloyalty, given time, hardens into open rebellion.

Absalom sends spies through the tribes: at the trumpet, proclaim him king. The conspiracy grows strong, and even David's counsellor Ahithophel joins it. A revolt long prepared bursts out at once. Quiet disloyalty, given time, hardens into open rebellion.

Word comes that all Israel's heart is with Absalom, and David flees Jerusalem on foot with his household, leaving the city rather than see it destroyed. The king who once fled Saul now flees his own son. Sin's harvest, foretold by Nathan, has fully come.

Word comes that all Israel's heart is with Absalom, and David flees Jerusalem on foot with his household, leaving the city rather than see it destroyed. The king who once fled Saul now flees his own son. Sin's harvest, foretold by Nathan, has fully come.

David's loyal servants and his foreign guard pass before him out of the city. In the day of trouble the true and the false are sorted out. Adversity reveals who will follow us into exile and who only flocked to us in prosperity.

David's loyal servants and his foreign guard pass before him out of the city. In the day of trouble the true and the false are sorted out. Adversity reveals who will follow us into exile and who only flocked to us in prosperity.

David urges Ittai the Gittite, a foreigner only newly come, to turn back and spare himself. But Ittai swears to stay: 'whithersoever my lord shall be, there also will thy servant be.' A stranger shows the devotion David's own son lacked. True loyalty cleaves closest in the darkest hour.

David urges Ittai the Gittite, a foreigner only newly come, to turn back and spare himself. But Ittai swears to stay: 'whithersoever my lord shall be, there also will thy servant be.' A stranger shows the devotion David's own son lacked. True loyalty cleaves closest in the darkest hour.

All the country weeps aloud as the king passes over the brook Kidron, and the priests come bearing the ark of God. Even in flight David is not without those who love him and the signs of God's presence. Grief and faith walk together out of the city.

All the country weeps aloud as the king passes over the brook Kidron, and the priests come bearing the ark of God. Even in flight David is not without those who love him and the signs of God's presence. Grief and faith walk together out of the city.

But David sends the ark back: 'if I shall find favour... he will bring me again'; and if not, 'let him do to me as seemeth good.' He will not use the ark as a charm to force God's hand, but submits wholly to His will. This is faith at its purest — surrendered, not bargaining.

But David sends the ark back: 'if I shall find favour... he will bring me again'; and if not, 'let him do to me as seemeth good.' He will not use the ark as a charm to force God's hand, but submits wholly to His will. This is faith at its purest — surrendered, not bargaining.

David sends Zadok and the priests back to be his eyes in the city, planting a quiet network of help. Submission to God's will does not forbid prudent action; David trusts God and uses wise means. Faith waits on God, yet still does what wisdom requires.

David climbs Olivet weeping, barefoot, head covered — and hearing Ahithophel has joined the revolt, prays simply, 'O LORD, turn the counsel of Ahithophel into foolishness.' In his lowest hour the king's first instinct is to pray. Tears and trust can climb the same hill together.

David climbs Olivet weeping, barefoot, head covered — and hearing Ahithophel has joined the revolt, prays simply, 'O LORD, turn the counsel of Ahithophel into foolishness.' In his lowest hour the king's first instinct is to pray. Tears and trust can climb the same hill together.

At the summit Hushai meets him, and David sends him back to feign loyalty to Absalom and defeat Ahithophel's counsel. The prayer of the moment before begins to be answered through a willing friend. God often works the deliverance we pray for through the people He sends across our path.

At the summit Hushai meets him, and David sends him back to feign loyalty to Absalom and defeat Ahithophel's counsel. The prayer of the moment before begins to be answered through a willing friend. God often works the deliverance we pray for through the people He sends across our path.

At the summit Hushai meets him, and David sends him back to feign loyalty to Absalom and defeat Ahithophel's counsel. The prayer of the moment before begins to be answered through a willing friend. God often works the deliverance we pray for through the people He sends across our path.

David arranges for Hushai to relay Absalom's plans through the priests to him, and Hushai enters the city just as Absalom arrives. Quietly the LORD is setting His pieces in place. Even as the rebellion looks triumphant, God is already weaving the threads that will unravel it.
